title: NOC 73300 – Transport truck drivers | GTR Immigration
description: NOC 73300 professionals are the backbone of Canada’s national and international trade. As a TEER 3 occupation, Transport Truck Drivers operate heavy trucks (weighing over 4500 kg) to move freight over long distances or local urban routes. In 2026, as Canada faces a critical labor shortage in the logistics sector, these workers are classified as High-Priority Essential Talent. For immigration, this role is a star candidate for the Express Entry Transport Category-Based Selection and high-volume Provincial Nominee Programs (PNP).

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category7 – Trades, transport and equipment operators and related occupationsTEER Level3Major Group73 – General tradesSub-Major Group733 – Transportation occupationsMinor Group7330 – Transportation occupationsNOC Code73300

duties: Trip Planning: Plan trip logistics and obtain required documentation to transport goods across provincial and international routes.
Vehicle Operation: Operate and drive primarily tractor-trailers, long-combination vehicles, and straight-body trucks over 4500 kg.
Safety Inspection: Perform mandatory pre-trip, en route, and post-trip inspections of vehicle systems, including tires, lights, brakes, and cold storage units.
Cargo Security: Ensure cargo is secured properly in accordance with safety requirements and follow strict procedures for transporting dangerous goods.
Permit Management: Obtain special permits and other documents required to transport oversized cargo or freight on international routes.
Data Recording: Record cargo information, hours of service, distance traveled, and fuel consumption using on-board electronic logging devices (ELDs).
Documentation Admin: Administer bills of lading and maintain manual or electronic logbooks to comply with federal hours-of-service regulations.
Communication: Maintain constant communication with dispatchers, other drivers, and customers using communication devices and on-board computers.
Emergency Repair: Perform minor emergency roadside repairs such as bulb replacements or adjusting equipment as safely possible.
Hazardous Transport: May transport hazardous products or dangerous goods, requiring strict adherence to TDG (Transportation of Dangerous Goods) certification.
employment_requirements: Completion of secondary school is usually required.

Licensing (Mandatory):Class 1 or A License: Required for long-combination vehicles (tractor-trailers). This is the "Gold Standard" for immigration.Class 3 or D License: Required for driving straight-body trucks.Air Brake Endorsement (Z): Required for all vehicles equipped with air brakes.
Class 1 or A License: Required for long-combination vehicles (tractor-trailers). This is the "Gold Standard" for immigration.
Class 3 or D License: Required for driving straight-body trucks.
Air Brake Endorsement (Z): Required for all vehicles equipped with air brakes.
MELT Program: Most provinces (ON, AB, SK, BC, MB) require Mandatory Entry-Level Training (MELT), which involves a set number of training hours before the road test.
TDG Certification: Required for drivers transporting hazardous materials.
A clean driving record (Abstract) and a medical fitness certificate are mandatory for all commercial drivers.
salary_prospects: Note: In 2026, long-haul drivers often earn by the kilometer (e.g., $0.55 - $0.70/km), which can translate to annual salaries between $65,000 and $95,000 inclusive of subsistence allowances.
job_outlook: The outlook is Favourable. Projections indicate that the shift toward automated platooning and green-hydrogen trucks will change the nature of driving, but the need for professional operators to manage complex urban navigation and cross-border logistics remains high. According to COPS, Retirements will be the single largest driver of openings, ensuring demand remains high through 2033.
labour_market_demand: The demand for transport truck drivers in 2026 is Critical and Widespread. In Ontario, Alberta, and Saskatchewan, the logistics sector is reporting tens of thousands of vacancies. According to 2026 Job Bank data, the expansion of e-commerce and the need for reliable supply chains have made truck driving the most sought-after trade for international recruitment. While the national outlook is "Stable," the "Replacement Demand" due to retirements is so high that the industry must attract over 20,000 new drivers annually to maintain current operations.
High Earning Potential: Experienced long-haul drivers often earn significant premiums, with some owner-operators netting over $120,000 annually.
Modern Fleet Technology: Work with the latest fuel-efficient tractors equipped with advanced ELDs (Electronic Logging Devices), GPS routing, and collision-avoidance systems.

Path to PR Certainty: This NOC is one of the few explicitly named in Express Entry Category-Based draws, making it a fast-track route to Permanent Residency for those with as little as 6 months of experience.
Diverse Routes: Choose between "Home Daily" local routes or "Trans-Continental" long-haul adventures across the Canada-US border.
pathways: Express Entry (Transport Category Selection): This NOC is a high-priority target for 2026 category-based draws with lower CRS requirements.
Saskatchewan SINP (Long-Haul Truck Driver Project): A dedicated project for drivers already working in Saskatchewan for 6 months on a work permit.
Alberta Opportunity Stream: Ideal for drivers with a valid job offer and local license currently working in Alberta.
Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP): A high-conversion route for drivers with a job offer from a designated Atlantic employer.
